               Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
           Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
           Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
           ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
           Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
           of the Census (Department of Commerce), State
           Employment Security Agencies, and State Depart-
           ments of Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of
           Labor Statistics. A brief description of the co-
           operative statistical programs of the BLS with
           these agencies is presented in the Explanatory
           Notes. The State agencies are listed on the inside
           back cover.                                                                        CALENDAR OF SPECIAL FEATURES

                        Employment and Earnings
                         Vol. 26        No. 5       May 1979

                        Editors: Chester L. Green, Gloria P. Green, Marc Weinstein



                                    Editor's Note

                                        Subsequent to the production of the March 1979 Current Population Survey (household survey)
                                    data, a minor error was discovered in the estimation process. The effect of this error is an overstate-
                                    ment of 60,000 (0.04 percent) in the civilian noninstitutional population 16 years and over. Although
                                    the error was corrected prior to the production of the April data, the use of a composite estimator
                                    in the CPS produced a carryover effect which resulted in an understatement of 43,000 (0.03 percent)
                                    in the April civilian noninstitutional population. As a consequence, the published statistics show an
                                    over-the-month growth in population that is 103,000 less than the correct population figures would
                                    have indicated. The impact of these effects on the employment and unemployment estimates for
                                    each month is negligible. There will be no further carryover effects in May and subsequent months.

                                                                     Explanatory Notes

                                 These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                 (A tables), Establishment Data (8, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                                 lished in Employment and Earnings.



                                                                             Introduction

                  The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two major            Employment
               sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from employers.

              COLLECTION                                                                  The data exclude proprietors, the self-employed, unpaid volunteer
                                                                                       or family workers, farm workers, and domestic workers in house-
                 Payroll reports provide current information on wage and salary        holds. Salaried officers of corporations are included. Government
              employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover in nonagricultural       employment covers only civilian employees, military personnel are
              establishments, by industry and geographic location.                     excluded. Employees of the Central Intelligence and National
                                                                                       Security Agencies are also excluded.
              Federal-State cooperation
                                                                                           Persons on establishment payrolls who are on paid sick leave
                  Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies, the re-          (when pay is received directly from the firm), on paid holiday or
              spondent fills out a single employment or labor turnover report-         paid vacation, or who-work during a part of, the pay period even
              ing form, which is then used for national, State, and area estimates.    though they are unemployed or on strike during the rest of the
              This eliminates duplicate reporting on the part of respondents, and      period are counted as employed. Not counted as employed are
              together with the use of identical techniques at the national and        persons who are laid off, on leave without pay, or on strike for the
              State levels, insures maximum comparability of estimates.                entire period or who are hired but have not been paid during the
                  State agencies mail the forms to the establishments and examine      period.
              the returns for consistency, accuracy, and completeness. The
              States use the information to prepare State and area series
              and then send the establishment data to the BLS (Washington              Industry hours and earnings
              Office) for use in preparing the national series.                           Average hours and earnings data are derived from reports of
                                                                                       payrolls and hours for production and related workers in manu-
              Shuttle schedules                                                        facturing and mining, construction workers in construction, and
                                                                                       nonsupervisory employees in the remaining private nonagricul-
